questions.question: A team complains that 'it works on my machine but breaks in production.' Which characteristic of containers most directly eliminates this problem?
options: Containers share the host OS kernel, so they start faster than VMs.
A container packages the application together with its libraries, runtime, and system tools into one standardized unit that runs the same way everywhere.
Containers are managed by Docker, which automatically synchronises code between developer laptops and production servers.
Containers use a hypervisor to isolate each application in its own guest operating system.
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The 'works on my machine' problem arises because environments differ — different OS versions, missing libraries, wrong runtime. A container solves this by bundling the application with everything it depends on, so the exact same image runs identically on a developer laptop and on a production server. Option A describes a performance advantage (fast start), not environment consistency. Option C is incorrect — Docker does not sync code between machines; that is Git's job. Option D describes a VM, not a container; containers share the host OS kernel rather than carrying their own guest OS.

questions.question: In the end-to-end DevOps toolchain described in this lecture, which role do containers (Docker images) play?
options: They store and version all configuration files, replacing the need for Git.
They are the universal unit of delivery that CI builds, CD ships, orchestration runs, and monitoring watches.
They replace virtual machines entirely and remove the need for a cloud provider.
They automate the deployment pipeline by reacting to a Git push.
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The lecture explicitly states that containers are 'the common currency that lets every other tool in the chain cooperate' — CI builds them, CD ships them, orchestration runs them, and monitoring watches them. They are the universal unit of delivery flowing through the entire pipeline. Option A is wrong because Git handles version control, not containers. Option C overstates the case; containers are lighter than VMs but cloud providers are still required to host the physical or virtual infrastructure. Option D describes what a CI/CD engine does, not what containers do.

questions.question: A service that handles Black Friday traffic needs to run 50 container replicas, and at 3 a.m. a crashed replica must be automatically restarted. Which tool category is responsible for these capabilities?
options: CI/CD engine (e.g., Jenkins or GitHub Actions)
Infrastructure as Code tool (e.g., Terraform)
Container orchestrator (e.g., Kubernetes)
Monitoring stack (e.g., Prometheus and Grafana)
questions.explanation: Option C is correct. Orchestration — exemplified by Kubernetes — handles scheduling containers across servers, scaling replicas up and down based on demand, and self-healing (automatically restarting failed containers). The lecture lists exactly these responsibilities under the 'When one server is not enough: orchestration' section. Option A (CI/CD) automates the build-test-deploy pipeline but does not manage running containers at scale. Option B (IaC) provisions and configures the underlying infrastructure, not the containers running on top of it. Option D (monitoring) observes and alerts but does not act to restart or scale containers.

questions.question: An attacker wipes out a team's entire cloud environment — every server, Kubernetes cluster, and networking configuration. Which tool category was designed specifically to make this disaster recoverable without weeks of manual rebuilding?
options: Monitoring and observability (Prometheus / Grafana) — because dashboards record the previous configuration.
Infrastructure as Code (Terraform / Ansible) — because the environment is described in files and can be recreated by running a script.
Container orchestration (Kubernetes) — because Kubernetes stores the desired state of the cluster.
CI/CD engine (Jenkins / GitHub Actions) — because pipelines automatically redeploy the last known-good image.
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The lecture describes IaC with the scenario of an environment 'wiped out by a misconfiguration or an attack' and explains that IaC prevents this nightmare because infrastructure is described in files: 'Run the script and the entire environment appears; run it again and you get an identical environment.' Terraform provisions cloud resources and Ansible configures the servers inside them. Option A is wrong — monitoring dashboards show what was running but cannot recreate destroyed infrastructure. Option C is partially relevant but Kubernetes only manages the containers layer; if the underlying servers and cluster are gone, Kubernetes cannot recreate them. Option D is also partially useful but a CI/CD pipeline can only redeploy application images onto existing infrastructure, not rebuild the infrastructure itself.

questions.question: According to the learning roadmap in this lecture, a DevOps beginner who has just finished this introductory course should learn Docker and Kubernetes at which stage?
options: First, before anything else, because containers are the foundation of modern DevOps.
Third, after building solid Linux/Git/scripting foundations and gaining basic cloud provider knowledge.
Fifth, after completing CI/CD pipelines and Infrastructure as Code.
Last, because monitoring and observability are more immediately useful.
questions.explanation: Option B is correct. The roadmap places Docker and Kubernetes at step 3: (1) Linux, Git, scripting; (2) one cloud provider (AWS); (3) Docker then Kubernetes; (4) CI/CD in practice; (5) IaC (Terraform, Ansible); (6) Monitoring. The reasoning given in the lecture is that containers run on Linux, Kubernetes worker nodes run Linux, and everything else will run on a cloud platform — so those foundations must come first. Option A is tempting but incorrect; jumping to containers without Linux/Git/cloud knowledge leaves gaps that slow down hands-on work. Options C and D place containers too late in the sequence according to the recommended roadmap.
